CREATE TABLE hdms=# hdms=# -- Service Requests table hdms=# CREATE TABLE service_requests ( hdms(# id VARCHAR(50) PRIMARY KEY, hdms(# requesterId VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL, hdms(# departmentId VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL, hdms(# status VARCHAR(20) NOT NULL CHECK (status IN ('PENDING','APPROVED','REJECTED','IN_PROGRESS','DONE')), hdms(# priority VARCHAR(20) NOT NULL CHECK (priority IN ('LOW','MEDIUM','HIGH','CRITICAL')), hdms(# category VARCHAR(255), hdms(# subject VARCHAR(500), hdms(# description TEXT, hdms(# rejectionReason TEXT, hdms(# createdAt TIMESTAMP DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP, hdms(# updatedAt TIMESTAMP DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP, hdms(# CONSTRAINT fk_requester FOREIGN KEY(requesterId) REFERENCES users(id) ON DELETE CASCADE, hdms(# CONSTRAINT fk_request_department FOREIGN KEY(departmentId) REFERENCES departments(id) ON DELETE CASCADE hdms(# ); CREATE TABLE hdms=# hdms=# -- Tasks table hdms=# CREATE TABLE tasks ( hdms(# id VARCHAR(50) PRIMARY KEY, hdms(# requestId VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL, hdms(# assigneeId VARCHAR(50), hdms(# status VARCHAR(20) NOT NULL CHECK (status IN ('OPEN','IN_PROGRESS','DONE','BLOCKED')), hdms(# resolutionNotes TEXT, hdms(# slaDeadline TIMESTAMP, hdms(# createdAt TIMESTAMP DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP, hdms(# updatedAt TIMESTAMP DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP, hdms(# CONSTRAINT fk_task_request FOREIGN KEY(requestId) REFERENCES service_requests(id) ON DELETE CASCADE, hdms(# CONSTRAINT fk_task_assignee FOREIGN KEY(assigneeId) REFERENCES users(id) ON DELETE SET NULL hdms(# ); CREATE TABLE hdms=# hdms=# -- Optional: Indexes to speed up lookups hdms=# CREATE INDEX idx_users_department ON users(departmentId); CREATE INDEX hdms=# CREATE INDEX idx_service_requests_status ON service_requests(status); CREATE INDEX hdms=# CREATE INDEX idx_tasks_status ON tasks(status); CREATE INDEX hdms=# hdms=# -- ============================== hdms=# -- End of HDMS Schema hdms=# -- ==============================-- List all tables hdms=# \dt List of relations Schema | Name | Type | Owner --------+------------------+-------+---------- public | departments | table | postgres public | service_requests | table | postgres public | tasks | table | postgres public | users | table | postgres (4 rows) hdms=# hdms=# -- Check table structure hdms=# \d users Table "public.users" Column | Type | Collation | Nullable | Default --------------+-----------------------------+-----------+----------+------------------- id | character varying(50) | | not null | email | character varying(255) | | not null | fullname | character varying(255) | | not null | role | character varying(20) | | not null | departmentid | character varying(50) | | | isactive | boolean | | | true createdat | timestamp without time zone | | | CURRENT_TIMESTAMP updatedat | timestamp without time zone | | | CURRENT_TIMESTAMP Indexes: "users_pkey" PRIMARY KEY, btree (id) "idx_users_department" btree (departmentid) "users_email_key" UNIQUE CONSTRAINT, btree (email) Check constraints: "users_role_check" CHECK (role::text = ANY (ARRAY['ADMIN'::character varying, 'MANAGER'::character varying, 'SUPPORT'::character varying, 'EMPLOYEE'::character varying]::text[])) Referenced by: TABLE "departments" CONSTRAINT "fk_manager" FOREIGN KEY (managerid) REFERENCES users(id) ON DELETE SET NULL TABLE "service_requests" CONSTRAINT "fk_requester" FOREIGN KEY (requesterid) REFERENCES users(id) ON DELETE CASCADE TABLE "tasks" CONSTRAINT "fk_task_assignee" FOREIGN KEY (assigneeid) REFERENCES users(id) ON DELETE SET NULL hdms=#

Perfect! ✅ Your database is fully set up, and all tables are created exactly as per your HDMS schema. You can see all tables (users, departments, service_requests, tasks) with proper primary keys, foreign keys, indexes, and constraints.

For example, \d users shows:

  • id as PRIMARY KEY

  • email as UNIQUE

  • role has CHECK constraint for allowed roles

  • departmentId linked in foreign keys in departments and service_requests

  • isActive defaults to TRUE

  • createdAt and updatedAt default to CURRENT_TIMESTAMP

Everything is ready to start inserting data and testing your app.

If you want, I can create a ready-to-run SQL seed script that:

  • Adds default users (Admin, IT Manager, Support Agent, Employee)

  • Adds a sample department

  • Adds a sample service request and task

This will let you immediately log in and test all workflows without manually entering data.

Do you want me to create that seed script next?


You said:

yes I want

ChatGPT said:

Perfect! Let's create a PostgreSQL seed script for your HDMS database that you can run immediately inside your Docker container. This will populate some default data for testing: users, a department, a service request, and a task.

Here’s the full SQL script:

SQL
-- ==============================
-- HDMS Seed Data
-- ==============================

-- Clear existing data (optional if starting fresh)
TRUNCATE TABLE tasks CASCADE;
TRUNCATE TABLE service_requests CASCADE;
TRUNCATE TABLE users CASCADE;
TRUNCATE TABLE departments CASCADE;

-- ------------------------------
-- Insert Departments
-- ------------------------------
INSERT INTO departments (id, name, managerId, description, createdAt, updatedAt)
VALUES
('dept-001', 'IT Department', NULL, 'Handles IT support and infrastructure', NOW(), NOW());

-- ------------------------------
-- Insert Users
-- ------------------------------
INSERT INTO users (id, email, fullName, role, departmentId, isActive, createdAt, updatedAt)
VALUES
('admin-001', 'admin@hdms.gov', 'Admin User', 'ADMIN', NULL, TRUE, NOW(), NOW()),
('manager-001', 'it.mgr@hdms.gov', 'IT Manager', 'MANAGER', 'dept-001', TRUE, NOW(), NOW()),
('support-001', 'it.staff1@hdms.gov', 'Support Agent', 'SUPPORT', 'dept-001', TRUE, NOW(), NOW()),
('employee-001', 'emp1@hdms.gov', 'Employee One', 'EMPLOYEE', 'dept-001', TRUE, NOW(), NOW());

-- Update department managerId to link to the manager
UPDATE departments SET managerId = 'manager-001' WHERE id = 'dept-001';

-- ------------------------------
-- Insert Service Requests
-- ------------------------------
INSERT INTO service_requests (id, requesterId, departmentId, status, priority, category, subject, description, createdAt, updatedAt)
VALUES
('req-001', 'employee-001', 'dept-001', 'PENDING', 'HIGH', 'Software', 'Computer not working', 'My laptop is not turning on.', NOW(), NOW());

-- ------------------------------
-- Insert Tasks
-- ------------------------------
INSERT INTO tasks (id, requestId, assigneeId, status, resolutionNotes, slaDeadline, createdAt, updatedAt)
VALUES
('task-001', 'req-001', 'support-001', 'OPEN', NULL, NOW() + INTERVAL '2 days', NOW(), NOW());

-- ==============================
-- End of Seed Data
-- ==============================

How to run this inside Docker:

  1. Save the SQL script to a file, e.g., seed_hdms.sql on your host machine.

  2. Copy the file into the container (optional):

Bash
docker cp seed_hdms.sql hdms-postgres:/seed_hdms.sql

  1. Execute it inside the container:

Bash
docker exec -i hdms-postgres psql -U postgres -d hdms -f /seed_hdms.sql

After running this, your HDMS database will have:

  • 1 department: IT Department

  • 4 users: Admin, Manager, Support Agent, Employee

  • 1 service request from the employee

  • 1 task assigned to the support agent

Your system will be ready for testing login, request creation, task handling, and dashboards.

If you want, I can also create a TypeORM-compatible seed script in TypeScript that you can run with npx ts-node so it’s fully aligned with your project codebase. This is better if you plan to automate seeding in dev environments.

Do you want me to do that too?


You said:

Hey look I asked the CODEX as it do each steps above you said for me and he dids the ff and says the ff:Here’s a ready-to-run TypeORM seed script aligned with your codebase. What I added New file src/database/seeds/seed.ts that: Initializes the datasource. Clears tables in FK-safe order (tasks → service_requests → users → departments). Inserts the department, four users, the service request, and the task with current timestamps. Updates the department manager link. Shuts down the datasource cleanly. How to run it (inside your project root) Make sure Postgres is up (e.g., your hdms-postgres container) and .env points to it. Run with ts-node (works with your ESM setup): npx ts-node src/database/seeds/seed.ts If PowerShell blocks scripts, prefix with npm.cmd exec ts-node .... After running Department: IT Department (dept-001) Users: admin-001, manager-001, support-001, employee-001 Service request: req-001 by employee-001 (PENDING, HIGH) Task: task-001 assigned to support-001 (OPEN, SLA +2 days) If you prefer pure SQL inside the container, you can still use the provided SQL via docker cp + docker exec ... psql -f /seed_hdms.sql; the dataset matches the script above.

ChatGPT said:

Perfect! That means everything is ready for your HDMS dev environment. Here’s a concise summary of what you have now and the next steps you can take:

✅ What you have

  1. Database running

    • PostgreSQL 15 in Docker container hdms-postgres.

    • Database hdms created and accessible (psql -U postgres -d hdms works).

  2. Tables created

    • users, departments, service_requests, tasks.

    • All foreign keys, constraints, and indexes are in place.

  3. Seed data script

    • TypeORM seed script: src/database/seeds/seed.ts

    • Inserts:

      • 1 department (IT Department → dept-001)

      • 4 users (Admin, Manager, Support Agent, Employee)

      • 1 service request (req-001 by employee-001)

      • 1 task (task-001 assigned to support-001)

    • Automatically sets timestamps and department manager link.

  4. How to run the seed

    Bash
    npx ts-node src/database/seeds/seed.ts

    • Makes your database ready with realistic test data.

    • Can also run a pure SQL version inside Docker if preferred.

⚡ Next steps you can do

  1. Test the system

    • Log in as admin@hdms.gov, it.mgr@hdms.gov, it.staff1@hdms.gov, or emp1@hdms.gov.

    • Check dashboards and role-based access.

  2. Add more realistic data

    • You can expand seed.ts or SQL to insert more users, departments, and requests for testing scalability.

  3. Automate seeding

    • Add npm run seed script to package.json for easy repetition during dev.

  4. Check relationships

    • Run queries like:

      SQL
      SELECT u.fullname, d.name AS department
      FROM users u
      LEFT JOIN departments d ON u.departmentId = d.id;

    • Verify FK constraints, cascade behavior, and indexes.

If you want, I can write the final seed.ts code exactly ready to paste in your project, fully TypeORM-typed, with FK-safe clear order, timestamps, and auto-linking manager—so you don’t need to manually tweak anything.

Do you want me to do that?


You said:

Still look I'm geting the ff errors:PS C:\Users\HP\Documents\INSA\hdms---help-desk-management-system (1)> npm run dev > react-example@0.0.0 dev > tsx server.ts [dotenv@17.3.1] injecting env (7) from .env -- tip: ⚙️ override existing env vars with { override: true } [HDMS] Initializing database connection... DB CONFIG: { host: 'host.docker.internal', port: '5432', user: 'postgres', pass: 'postgres', db: 'hdms' } [HDMS] Database connection failed: error: password authentication failed for user "postgres" at parseErrorMessage (C:\Users\HP\Documents\INSA\hdms---help-desk-management-system (1)\node_modules\pg-protocol\src\parser.ts:394:9) at Parser.handlePacket (C:\Users\HP\Documents\INSA\hdms---help-desk-management-system (1)\node_modules\pg-protocol\src\parser.ts:212:19) at Parser.parse (C:\Users\HP\Documents\INSA\hdms---help-desk-management-system (1)\node_modules\pg-protocol\src\parser.ts:105:30) at Socket.<anonymous> (C:\Users\HP\Documents\INSA\hdms---help-desk-management-system (1)\node_modules\pg-protocol\src\index.ts:7:48) at Socket.emit (node:events:508:28) at addChunk (node:internal/streams/readable:559:12) at readableAddChunkPushByteMode (node:internal/streams/readable:510:3) at Readable.push (node:internal/streams/readable:390:5) at TCP.onStreamRead (node:internal/stream_base_commons:189:23) { length: 104, severity: 'FATAL', code: '28P01', detail: undefined, hint: undefined, position: undefined, internalPosition: undefined, internalQuery: undefined, where: undefined, schema: undefined, table: undefined, column: undefined, dataType: undefined, constraint: undefined, file: 'auth.c', line: '320', routine: 'auth_failed' } [HDMS] Falling back to in-memory storage (development mode) [HDMS] Identity Service Running on http://localhost:3000 [HDMS] Environment: development

ChatGPT said:

Ah — now I see exactly what’s happening. Your database container is up and running, but your app cannot authenticate. That 28P01 error is PostgreSQL’s “password authentication failed” for user postgres. Let’s fix it step by step.

1️⃣ Check the credentials inside the container

Run:

Bash
docker exec -it hdms-postgres psql -U postgres -d hdms

Then inside psql, run:

SQL
\du

This lists all users and their roles. You should see postgres as a superuser.

Next, verify/change the password:

SQL
ALTER USER postgres WITH PASSWORD 'postgres';

This ensures the password matches what’s in your .env.

Then exit psql:

SQL
\q

2️⃣ Update your .env

Make sure your .env in the project root matches exactly the database:

env
DB_HOST=host.docker.internal   # On Windows with Docker Desktop, or localhost if connecting directly
DB_PORT=5432
DB_USERNAME=postgres
DB_PASSWORD=postgres
DB_NAME=hdms

⚠️ Important: DB_USERNAME must match the PostgreSQL username (postgres) and DB_PASSWORD must match exactly what you set in step 1.

3️⃣ Restart your app

After updating .env, stop your server completely and run:

Bash
npm run dev

You should see:

[HDMS] Database connected successfully

instead of falling back to in-memory storage.

4️⃣ Notes on host.docker.internal

  • Windows + Docker Desktop: host.docker.internal is correct when your Node.js app runs on Windows and connects to a containerized Postgres.

  • If Node.js is inside a container too, use the container name hdms-postgres as DB_HOST.

✅ Optional debug step

If it still fails, you can test connectivity from your host:

Bash
psql -h host.docker.internal -U postgres -d hdms

  • If you don’t have psql installed locally, you can use:

Bash
docker exec -it hdms-postgres psql -U postgres -d hdms

This confirms the password works and the DB is accessible.

I can write you a ready .env + PowerShell command combo that ensures Node.js can connect to your Docker Postgres without this 28P01 error.

Do you want me to do that?


You said:

hey where do I run:docker exec -it hdms-postgres psql -U postgres -d hdms

ChatGPT said:

You run that command in your host machine’s terminal, the same place you’ve been running Docker commands — in your case, PowerShell.

Step by step:

  1. Open PowerShell (you’re already in C:\Users\HP\Documents\INSA\hdms---help-desk-management-system (1)).

  2. Type:

PowerShell
docker exec -it hdms-postgres psql -U postgres -d hdms

  1. Press Enter.

If it works, you’ll see the psql prompt:

hdms=#

From there you can run SQL commands, e.g.:

SQL
\du                 -- list users
ALTER USER postgres WITH PASSWORD 'postgres';  -- reset password
\q                  -- exit psql

⚠️ Important: Make sure your Docker container is running (docker ps to check). If it’s not, start it:

PowerShell
docker start hdms-postgres

Once done, your .env credentials (DB_USERNAME and DB_PASSWORD) should match exactly what you set here.

I can give you the exact .env setup for your Windows + Docker configuration so your Node.js app connects without errors. Do you want me to do that next?


You said:

PS C:\Users\HP\Documents\INSA\hdms---help-desk-management-system (1)> docker exec -it hdms-postgres psql -U postgres -d hdms psql (15.17) Type "help" for help. hdms=# \du -- list users List of roles Role name | Attributes | Member of -----------+------------+----------- \du: extra argument "list" ignored \du: extra argument "users" ignored hdms=# ALTER USER postgres WITH PASSWORD 'postgres'; -- reset password ALTER ROLE hdms=# \q -- exit psql \q: extra argument "--" ignored \q: extra argument "exit" ignored \q: extra argument "psql" ignored PS C:\Users\HP\Documents\INSA\hdms---help-desk-management-system (1)> docker start hdms-postgres hdms-postgres PS C:\Users\HP\Documents\INSA\hdms---help-desk-management-system (1)>

ChatGPT said:

Perfect! ✅ You just reset the postgres password and ensured your Docker container is running.

Now let’s make sure your .env matches exactly what your Postgres container expects:

env
# Database
DB_HOST=host.docker.internal   # use 'localhost' if running Node app inside the same host
DB_PORT=5432
DB_USERNAME=postgres
DB_PASSWORD=postgres
DB_NAME=hdms

# API
VITE_API_URL=http://localhost:3000/api

# App
NODE_ENV=development

Steps to apply:

  1. Save the above in your .env file.

  2. Restart your Node.js dev server:

PowerShell
npm run dev

You should now see:

[HDMS] Database connected successfully

No fallback to in-memory storage.

💡 Tip: If you still get password errors, double-check:

  • Your Node app is running on the same machine as Docker, otherwise use DB_HOST=localhost or the actual container IP.

  • There are no extra spaces in .env.

  • Docker container is actually running: docker ps → should show hdms-postgres.

Do you want me to show a quick test command to verify Node can connect to Postgres before running the full app?


You said:

Here all full files of my .env:DB_HOST="host.docker.internal" DB_PORT="5432" DB_USERNAME="postgres" DB_PASSWORD="postgres" DB_NAME="hdms" VITE_API_URL="http://localhost:3000/api" GEMINI_API_KEY="MY_GEMINI_API_KEY"

ChatGPT said:

Ah! I see the issue. Your .env file uses quotes around all values:

env
DB_HOST="host.docker.internal"
DB_PORT="5432"
DB_USERNAME="postgres"
DB_PASSWORD="postgres"
DB_NAME="hdms"

Some Node.js libraries (like dotenv + TypeORM/pg) interpret quotes literally, so the password is actually "postgres" instead of postgres. This will cause the password authentication failed error.

✅ Fix: Remove the quotes for all database-related variables:

env
DB_HOST=host.docker.internal
DB_PORT=5432
DB_USERNAME=postgres
DB_PASSWORD=postgres
DB_NAME=hdms

VITE_API_URL=http://localhost:3000/api
GEMINI_API_KEY=MY_GEMINI_API_KEY

After that:

  1. Save .env.

  2. Stop the dev server if running: Ctrl+C

  3. Run it again:

PowerShell
npm run dev

You should see:

[HDMS] Database connected successfully
[HDMS] Identity Service Running on http://localhost:3000

This is the most common cause for password errors when using Docker + Postgres + Node.

If you want, I can also give a quick test command inside Node to confirm the DB connection before running the full app—so you don’t waste time debugging later. Do you want me to do that?


You said:

PS C:\Users\HP\Documents\INSA\hdms---help-desk-management-system (1)> npm run dev > react-example@0.0.0 dev > tsx server.ts [dotenv@17.3.1] injecting env (7) from .env -- tip: 🛠️ run anywhere with dotenvx run -- yourcommand [HDMS] Initializing database connection... DB CONFIG: { host: 'host.docker.internal', port: '5432', user: 'postgres', pass: 'postgres', db: 'hdms' } [HDMS] Database connection failed: error: password authentication failed for user "postgres" at parseErrorMessage (C:\Users\HP\Documents\INSA\hdms---help-desk-management-system (1)\node_modules\pg-protocol\src\parser.ts:394:9) at Parser.handlePacket (C:\Users\HP\Documents\INSA\hdms---help-desk-management-system (1)\node_modules\pg-protocol\src\parser.ts:212:19) at Parser.parse (C:\Users\HP\Documents\INSA\hdms---help-desk-management-system (1)\node_modules\pg-protocol\src\parser.ts:105:30) at Socket.<anonymous> (C:\Users\HP\Documents\INSA\hdms---help-desk-management-system (1)\node_modules\pg-protocol\src\index.ts:7:48) at Socket.emit (node:events:508:28) at addChunk (node:internal/streams/readable:559:12) at readableAddChunkPushByteMode (node:internal/streams/readable:510:3) at Readable.push (node:internal/streams/readable:390:5) at TCP.onStreamRead (node:internal/stream_base_commons:189:23) { length: 104, severity: 'FATAL', code: '28P01', detail: undefined, hint: undefined, position: undefined, internalPosition: undefined, internalQuery: undefined, where: undefined, schema: undefined, table: undefined, column: undefined, dataType: undefined, constraint: undefined, file: 'auth.c', line: '320', routine: 'auth_failed' } [HDMS] Falling back to in-memory storage (development mode) [HDMS] Identity Service Running on http://localhost:3000 [HDMS] Environment: development

ChatGPT said:

Got it — the error persists because PostgreSQL inside Docker and your Node app still aren’t agreeing on the password. Let’s systematically fix it.

1️⃣ Verify Postgres password inside Docker

Run:

PowerShell
docker exec -it hdms-postgres psql -U postgres -d hdms

Then, inside psql, reset the password explicitly:

SQL
ALTER USER postgres WITH PASSWORD 'postgres';
\q

You already did this, but just to confirm it’s still applied.

2️⃣ Confirm .env formatting

Make sure no quotes exist around values:

env
DB_HOST=host.docker.internal
DB_PORT=5432
DB_USERNAME=postgres
DB_PASSWORD=postgres
DB_NAME=hdms

VITE_API_URL=http://localhost:3000/api
GEMINI_API_KEY=MY_GEMINI_API_KEY

Important: Node reads "postgres" (with quotes) literally. That breaks authentication.

3️⃣ Confirm your Node app can reach the Docker host

Since you’re on Windows, host.docker.internal is correct. But we need to make sure the container is running and accepting connections:

PowerShell
docker ps

You should see hdms-postgres running and 0.0.0.0:5432->5432/tcp.

Then test direct connection from your host using psql:

PowerShell
docker exec -it hdms-postgres psql -U postgres -d hdms
# inside psql:
\conninfo

Or install psql locally and run:

PowerShell
psql -h host.docker.internal -U postgres -d hdms

You should be prompted for a password. Enter postgres. If this fails, the password isn’t synced correctly.

4️⃣ Remove old Docker container (if needed)

Sometimes the Postgres container keeps an old password in its volume. If authentication keeps failing:

PowerShell
docker stop hdms-postgres
docker rm hdms-postgres
docker volume prune  # WARNING: deletes unused volumes
docker run --name hdms-postgres -e POSTGRES_USER=postgres -e POSTGRES_PASSWORD=postgres -e POSTGRES_DB=hdms -p 5432:5432 -d postgres:15-alpine

Then try again from Node.

5️⃣ Restart your dev server

After confirming Postgres password works:

PowerShell
npm run dev

If everything is correct, you should see:

[HDMS] Database connected successfully
[HDMS] Identity Service Running on http://localhost:3000

💡 Extra tip: For testing DB credentials in Node without running the full server:

Create a small test-db.ts:

TypeScript
import { Client } from 'pg';
import dotenv from 'dotenv';

dotenv.config();

const client = new Client({
  host: process.env.DB_HOST,
  port: Number(process.env.DB_PORT),
  user: process.env.DB_USERNAME,
  password: process.env.DB_PASSWORD,
  database: process.env.DB_NAME,
});

async function test() {
  try {
    await client.connect();
    console.log('✅ DB connected!');
  } catch (err) {
    console.error('❌ DB connection failed:', err);
  } finally {
    await client.end();
  }
}

test();

Run:

PowerShell
npx ts-node test-db.ts

This will tell you immediately if the DB credentials are working.

If you want, I can write the exact sequence of commands to fully reset your Docker Postgres + Node connection so that npm run dev works without falling back to in-memory storage. This avoids the password issue entirely.

Do you want me to do that?


You said:

PS C:\Users\HP\Documents\INSA\hdms---help-desk-management-system (1)> docker exec -it hdms-postgres psql -U postgres -d hdms psql (15.17) Type "help" for help. hdms=# ALTER USER postgres WITH PASSWORD 'postgres'; ALTER ROLE hdms=# \q PS C:\Users\HP\Documents\INSA\hdms---help-desk-management-system (1)> docker ps CONTAINER ID IMAGE COMMAND CREATED STATUS PORTS NAMES 507ae2e4b6fe postgres:15-alpine "docker-entrypoint.s…" 37 minutes ago Up 37 minutes 0.0.0.0:5432->5432/tcp, [::]:5432->5432/tcp hdms-postgres 9e5bb80c9683 docker/welcome-to-docker:latest "/docker-entrypoint.…" 3 hours ago Up 3 hours 80/tcp sweet_dubinsky 916646d6e5c4 docker/welcome-to-docker:latest "/docker-entrypoint.…" 3 hours ago Up 3 hours 0.0.0.0:8088->80/tcp, [::]:8088->80/tcp welcome-to-docker PS C:\Users\HP\Documents\INSA\hdms---help-desk-management-system (1)> docker exec -it hdms-postgres psql -U postgres -d hdms psql (15.17) Type "help" for help. hdms=#

ChatGPT said:

Perfect — your Postgres container is up and the password has been reset correctly. ✅

Now let’s make sure your Node app can connect without falling back to in-memory storage. Follow these steps:

1️⃣ Fix your .env

Open your .env file and make sure no quotes around the values. It should look exactly like this:

env
DB_HOST=host.docker.internal
DB_PORT=5432
DB_USERNAME=postgres
DB_PASSWORD=postgres
DB_NAME=hdms

VITE_API_URL=http://localhost:3000/api
GEMINI_API_KEY=MY_GEMINI_API_KEY

Node reads "postgres" (with quotes) literally, which breaks authentication.

2️⃣ Test DB credentials from Node

Create a small test file test-db.ts in your project root:

TypeScript
import { Client } from 'pg';
import dotenv from 'dotenv';

dotenv.config();

const client = new Client({
  host: process.env.DB_HOST,
  port: Number(process.env.DB_PORT),
  user: process.env.DB_USERNAME,
  password: process.env.DB_PASSWORD,
  database: process.env.DB_NAME,
});

async function test() {
  try {
    await client.connect();
    console.log('✅ DB connected!');
  } catch (err) {
    console.error('❌ DB connection failed:', err);
  } finally {
    await client.end();
  }
}

test();

Run:

PowerShell
npx ts-node test-db.ts

  • If it prints ✅ DB connected!, your credentials are correct.

  • If it fails, the Node app still can’t reach Docker Postgres, or the password isn’t synced.

3️⃣ Restart your dev server

After confirming DB credentials work:

PowerShell
npm run dev

You should now see:

[HDMS] Database connected successfully
[HDMS] Identity Service Running on http://localhost:3000

No fallback to in-memory storage.

4️⃣ Optional: If it still fails

Sometimes Windows + Docker + Node can be tricky. If host.docker.internal doesn’t work:

  1. Find your Docker container IP:

PowerShell
docker inspect -f "{{range .NetworkSettings.Networks}}{{.IPAddress}}{{end}}" hdms-postgres

  1. Use that IP in .env instead of host.docker.internal:

env
DB_HOST=172.18.0.2  # replace with actual IP

Then retry npx ts-node test-db.ts.

If you want, I can give you a ready-to-run full command sequence that guarantees your Node + Docker Postgres connection works on Windows, including .env fixes, password reset, and container restart.

PS C:\Users\HP\Documents\INSA\hdms---help-desk-management-system (1)> docker exec -it hdms-postgres psql -U postgres -d hdms psql (15.17) Type "help" for help. hdms=# npx ts-node test-db.ts hdms-# npx ts-node test-db.ts hdms-#

ChatGPT said:

Ah — I see exactly what happened. 😅

npx ts-node test-db.ts is a Node command, not a SQL command. You don’t run it inside psql. Right now you’re inside the Postgres prompt (hdms=#) and it’s treating your command as SQL, which is why it just hangs waiting for a semicolon.

✅ Correct Steps

  1. Exit Postgres prompt:

SQL
\q

Now your PowerShell prompt should return:

PowerShell
PS C:\Users\HP\Documents\INSA\hdms---help-desk-management-system (1)>

  1. Run the Node test script from PowerShell, not inside psql:

PowerShell
npx ts-node test-db.ts

This will attempt to connect to your Docker Postgres and print whether it works.

Once that test succeeds (✅ DB connected!), you can then safely run:

PowerShell
npm run dev

and it should connect to the DB instead of falling back to in-memory storage.

If you want, I can also give you the exact full PowerShell sequence for Windows to reset the password, verify the DB, and start the dev server so you don’t hit this issue again.

You said:

PS C:\Users\HP\Documents\INSA\hdms---help-desk-management-system (1)> npx ts-node test-db.ts Need to install the following packages: ts-node@10.9.2 Ok to proceed? (y) y [dotenv@17.3.1] injecting env (7) from .env -- tip: 🔐 encrypt with Dotenvx: https://dotenvx.com ❌ DB connection failed: error: no pg_hba.conf entry for host "192.168.43.184", user "postgres", database "hdms", no encryption at parseErrorMessage (C:\Users\HP\Documents\INSA\hdms---help-desk-management-system (1)\node_modules\pg-protocol\src\parser.ts:394:9) at Parser.handlePacket (C:\Users\HP\Documents\INSA\hdms---help-desk-management-system (1)\node_modules\pg-protocol\src\parser.ts:212:19) at Parser.parse (C:\Users\HP\Documents\INSA\hdms---help-desk-management-system (1)\node_modules\pg-protocol\src\parser.ts:105:30) at Socket.<anonymous> (C:\Users\HP\Documents\INSA\hdms---help-desk-management-system (1)\node_modules\pg-protocol\src\index.ts:7:48) at Socket.emit (node:events:508:28) at Socket.emit (node:domain:489:12) at addChunk (node:internal/streams/readable:559:12) at readableAddChunkPushByteMode (node:internal/streams/readable:510:3) at Socket.Readable.push (node:internal/streams/readable:390:5) at TCP.onStreamRead (node:internal/stream_base_commons:189:23) { length: 158, severity: 'FATAL', code: '28000', detail: undefined, hint: undefined, position: undefined, internalPosition: undefined, internalQuery: undefined, where: undefined, schema: undefined, table: undefined, column: undefined, dataType: undefined, constraint: undefined, file: 'auth.c', line: '537', routine: 'ClientAuthentication' } PS C:\Users\HP\Documents\INSA\hdms---help-desk-management-system (1)>

ChatGPT said:

Ah! Now the error is different — it’s not your password, it’s PostgreSQL rejecting connections from your Windows host IP:

no pg_hba.conf entry for host "192.168.43.184", user "postgres", database "hdms", no encryption

This means PostgreSQL’s authentication configuration (pg_hba.conf) doesn’t allow your host machine to connect remotely.

How to fix this

Since you are using Docker:

1️⃣ Stop and remove the container (we’ll recreate it with proper settings):

PowerShell
docker stop hdms-postgres
docker rm hdms-postgres

2️⃣ Run Postgres container with proper host access:

PowerShell
docker run --name hdms-postgres `
  -e POSTGRES_USER=postgres `
  -e POSTGRES_PASSWORD=postgres `
  -e POSTGRES_DB=hdms `
  -p 5432:5432 `
  -d postgres:15-alpine

Note: Backticks (```) are PowerShell line continuations. You can also write this as one line without them.

This will expose Postgres to all host IPs through port 5432.

3️⃣ Allow all IPs in pg_hba.conf (inside container)

Enter container:

PowerShell
docker exec -it hdms-postgres sh

Edit or append pg_hba.conf:

Bash
echo "host all all 0.0.0.0/0 md5" >> /var/lib/postgresql/data/pg_hba.conf

Restart Postgres inside container:

Bash
pg_ctl -D /var/lib/postgresql/data restart

On alpine image, you might need:

Bash
kill -HUP $(head -1 /var/lib/postgresql/data/postmaster.pid)

4️⃣ Verify .env file uses host.docker.internal (as you already have):

env
DB_HOST=host.docker.internal
DB_PORT=5432
DB_USERNAME=postgres
DB_PASSWORD=postgres
DB_NAME=hdms

5️⃣ Test connection from Node:

PowerShell
npx ts-node test-db.ts

It should now connect successfully.

💡 Why this happened:

  • Docker Postgres listens on container IPs by default. Windows tries to connect via your network IP 192.168.43.184, which Postgres blocks unless explicitly allowed in pg_hba.conf.

If you want, I can give you a one-shot Docker command that spins up Postgres fully ready for your HDMS app, so you never hit this pg_hba.conf issue again.

Do you want me to do that?


You said:

PS C:\Users\HP\Documents\INSA\hdms---help-desk-management-system (1)> docker stop hdms-postgres hdms-postgres PS C:\Users\HP\Documents\INSA\hdms---help-desk-management-system (1)> docker rm hdms-postgres hdms-postgres PS C:\Users\HP\Documents\INSA\hdms---help-desk-management-system (1)> docker run --name hdms-postgres >> -e POSTGRES_USER=postgres >> -e POSTGRES_PASSWORD=postgres >> -e POSTGRES_DB=hdms >> -p 5432:5432 >> -d postgres:15-alpine 174df6bcbd0637d851e6a88e0e81f07c2b4eb6d19b2a93c1868195b94500874a PS C:\Users\HP\Documents\INSA\hdms---help-desk-management-system (1)> docker exec -it hdms-postgres sh / #

ChatGPT said:

Great! You’re now inside the Postgres container shell (sh). ✅

Next, we need to allow connections from your Windows host by updating pg_hba.conf.

1️⃣ Edit pg_hba.conf to allow all hosts

Since this is the Alpine image, the Postgres data directory is usually /var/lib/postgresql/data. Run:

Bash
echo "host all all 0.0.0.0/0 md5" >> /var/lib/postgresql/data/pg_hba.conf

This appends a line allowing all IPs to connect with password authentication.

2️⃣ Allow Postgres to listen on all interfaces

Open or create postgresql.conf (usually in same data directory):

Bash
echo "listen_addresses='*'" >> /var/lib/postgresql/data/postgresql.conf

3️⃣ Restart Postgres inside the container

Bash
pg_ctl -D /var/lib/postgresql/data restart

If it fails on Alpine, you can instead stop and start the container:

PowerShell
docker stop hdms-postgres
docker start hdms-postgres

4️⃣ Exit container

Bash
exit

5️⃣ Test connection from Node.js

Now, try:

PowerShell
npx ts-node test-db.ts

It should connect successfully using your .env credentials.

If you want, I can give you a single ready-to-run Docker command that spins up Postgres with host access, already bypassing all these pg_hba.conf steps. This way, you can reset and start fresh in one command.

Do you want me to do that?


You said:

PS C:\Users\HP\Documents\INSA\hdms---help-desk-management-system (1)> docker exec -it hdms-postgres sh / # echo "host all all 0.0.0.0/0 md5" >> /var/lib/postgresql/data/pg_hba.conf / # echo "listen_addresses='*'" >> /var/lib/postgresql/data/postgresql.conf / # pg_ctl -D /var/lib/postgresql/data restart pg_ctl: cannot be run as root Please log in (using, e.g., "su") as the (unprivileged) user that will own the server process. / #

ChatGPT said:

Ah! That explains the error. The Alpine Postgres container runs Postgres as the postgres user, not as root. That’s why pg_ctl failed. You have two practical options here:

Option 1: Restart the container (simplest)

Since the config changes are already written, just exit the container and restart it from your host:

PowerShell
exit
docker stop hdms-postgres
docker start hdms-postgres

Postgres will read the updated pg_hba.conf and postgresql.conf on startup.

Option 2: Run commands inside container as postgres user

You can switch to the correct user before restarting Postgres:

Bash
su - postgres
pg_ctl -D /var/lib/postgresql/data restart

But in most Docker setups, just restarting the container (Option 1) is enough.

✅ After restart

Try running your Node.js test again:

PowerShell
npx ts-node test-db.ts

It should now connect without the no pg_hba.conf entry error.

If you want, I can give you a clean Docker run command that spins up Postgres fully ready for your Node.js app—no manual config edits needed. This usually saves a lot of headaches.

Do you want me to do that?


You said:

Heeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeee:PS C:\Users\HP\Documents\INSA\hdms---help-desk-management-system (1)> docker stop hdms-postgres hdms-postgres PS C:\Users\HP\Documents\INSA\hdms---help-desk-management-system (1)> docker rm hdms-postgres hdms-postgres PS C:\Users\HP\Documents\INSA\hdms---help-desk-management-system (1)> docker run --name hdms-postgres >> -e POSTGRES_USER=postgres >> -e POSTGRES_PASSWORD=postgres >> -e POSTGRES_DB=hdms >> -p 5432:5432 >> -d postgres:15-alpine 174df6bcbd0637d851e6a88e0e81f07c2b4eb6d19b2a93c1868195b94500874a PS C:\Users\HP\Documents\INSA\hdms---help-desk-management-system (1)> docker exec -it hdms-postgres sh / # echo "host all all 0.0.0.0/0 md5" >> /var/lib/postgresql/data/pg_hba.conf / # echo "listen_addresses='*'" >> /var/lib/postgresql/data/postgresql.conf / # pg_ctl -D /var/lib/postgresql/data restart pg_ctl: cannot be run as root Please log in (using, e.g., "su") as the (unprivileged) user that will own the server process. / # exit PS C:\Users\HP\Documents\INSA\hdms---help-desk-management-system (1)> docker stop hdms-postgres hdms-postgres PS C:\Users\HP\Documents\INSA\hdms---help-desk-management-system (1)> docker start hdms-postgres hdms-postgres PS C:\Users\HP\Documents\INSA\hdms---help-desk-management-system (1)> npx ts-node test-db.ts [dotenv@17.3.1] injecting env (7) from .env -- tip: ⚙️ suppress all logs with { quiet: true } ❌ DB connection failed: error: no pg_hba.conf entry for host "192.168.43.184", user "postgres", database "hdms", no encryption at parseErrorMessage (C:\Users\HP\Documents\INSA\hdms---help-desk-management-system (1)\node_modules\pg-protocol\src\parser.ts:394:9) at Parser.handlePacket (C:\Users\HP\Documents\INSA\hdms---help-desk-management-system (1)\node_modules\pg-protocol\src\parser.ts:212:19) at Parser.parse (C:\Users\HP\Documents\INSA\hdms---help-desk-management-system (1)\node_modules\pg-protocol\src\parser.ts:105:30) at Socket.<anonymous> (C:\Users\HP\Documents\INSA\hdms---help-desk-management-system (1)\node_modules\pg-protocol\src\index.ts:7:48) at Socket.emit (node:events:508:28) at Socket.emit (node:domain:489:12) at addChunk (node:internal/streams/readable:559:12) at readableAddChunkPushByteMode (node:internal/streams/readable:510:3) at Socket.Readable.push (node:internal/streams/readable:390:5) at TCP.onStreamRead (node:internal/stream_base_commons:189:23) { length: 158, severity: 'FATAL', code: '28000', detail: undefined, hint: undefined, position: undefined, internalPosition: undefined, internalQuery: undefined, where: undefined, schema: undefined, table: undefined, column: undefined, dataType: undefined, constraint: undefined, file: 'auth.c', line: '537', routine: 'ClientAuthentication' } PS C:\Users\HP\Documents\INSA\hdms---help-desk-management-system (1)>

ChatGPT said:

Ah! Now it’s clear why you’re still hitting the no pg_hba.conf entry for host error. The problem isn’t your password anymore—it’s that Postgres inside the container is not allowing connections from your host machine. Writing to pg_hba.conf manually inside the container doesn’t take effect because Postgres runs as the postgres user and the container didn’t reload the configuration correctly.

Let’s fix this cleanly using Docker only so you won’t need manual edits.

Clean fix: Run Postgres container with host access allowed

  1. Stop and remove your old container:

PowerShell
docker stop hdms-postgres
docker rm hdms-postgres

  1. Run a new Postgres container allowing external connections:

PowerShell
docker run --name hdms-postgres `
  -e POSTGRES_USER=postgres `
  -e POSTGRES_PASSWORD=postgres `
  -e POSTGRES_DB=hdms `
  -p 5432:5432 `
  -d postgres:15-alpine `
  -c listen_addresses='*' `
  -c password_encryption='scram-sha-256'

✅ What this does:

  • listen_addresses='*' → allows connections from any host.

  • password_encryption='scram-sha-256' → modern password method; works with Node.js clients.

  • Environment variables set user/password/database correctly.

  1. Allow your host to connect:

Since postgres:alpine now listens on all interfaces, your Node.js app at host.docker.internal can connect directly. You don’t need to touch pg_hba.conf manually.

  1. Test with psql from host:

PowerShell
psql -h host.docker.internal -p 5432 -U postgres -d hdms

If it connects, your Node.js app should work too.

  1. Run your test script:

PowerShell
npx ts-node test-db.ts

It should now succeed.

If you want, I can write you the exact .env + Docker setup that will work perfectly for your HDMS project without ever touching pg_hba.conf manually, so you can just spin everything up and it’ll connect immediately.

Do you want me to do that?


You said:

PS C:\Users\HP\Documents\INSA\hdms---help-desk-management-system (1)> docker stop hdms-postgres hdms-postgres PS C:\Users\HP\Documents\INSA\hdms---help-desk-management-system (1)> docker rm hdms-postgres hdms-postgres PS C:\Users\HP\Documents\INSA\hdms---help-desk-management-system (1)> docker run --name hdms-postgres >> -e POSTGRES_USER=postgres >> -e POSTGRES_PASSWORD=postgres >> -e POSTGRES_DB=hdms >> -p 5432:5432 >> -d postgres:15-alpine >> -c listen_addresses='*' >> -c password_encryption='scram-sha-256' 5479bb1a307eeda69d65bc803bc5e627bb7e71fac599bcfd6aa1396a59b3c8dd PS C:\Users\HP\Documents\INSA\hdms---help-desk-management-system (1)> psql -h host.docker.internal -p 5432 -U postgres -d hdms psql : The term 'psql' is not recognized as the name of a cmdlet, function, script file, or operable program. Check the spelling of the name, or if a path was included, verify that the path is correct and try again. At line:1 char:1 + psql -h host.docker.internal -p 5432 -U postgres -d hdms + ~~~~ + CategoryInfo : ObjectNotFound: (psql:String) [], CommandNotFound Exception + FullyQualifiedErrorId : CommandNotFoundException

ChatGPT said:

Ah! That error just means Windows PowerShell doesn’t know the psql command—Postgres CLI isn’t installed on your host machine. That’s fine; we can still test and fix your connection in two ways:

Option 1: Use Docker to run psql

Since Postgres is running in Docker, you can run the CLI inside a temporary container:

PowerShell
docker run -it --rm --network host.docker.internal --link hdms-postgres:postgres postgres:15-alpine \
    psql -h hdms-postgres -U postgres -d hdms

  • --link hdms-postgres:postgres → lets this temporary container see your running DB container as postgres.

  • -it → interactive mode.

  • --rm → auto-delete container after exit.

Once it asks for a password, enter postgres.

If this works, your DB is accessible and your Node.js/TypeORM connection should work.

Option 2: Install psql locally on Windows

  1. Install Postgres for Windows (only CLI tools if you want):

    • Download from: https://www.postgresql.org/download/windows/

    • During install, select Command Line Tools only if you don’t need the full Postgres server.

  2. Add psql to your PATH (installer usually does this).

  3. Then run:

PowerShell
psql -h host.docker.internal -p 5432 -U postgres -d hdms

Password: postgres.

✅ Tip: For your Node.js app, as long as DB_HOST=host.docker.internal and the container is listening on all addresses (listen_addresses='*'), it should connect without psql.

The psql test is just to confirm connectivity.

If you want, I can give you a ready-to-run Node.js + Docker + .env setup for HDMS that will connect immediately, so you can skip all pg_hba.conf headaches.

Do you want me to do that?


You said:

PS C:\Users\HP\Documents\INSA\hdms---help-desk-management-system (1)> docker run -it --rm --network host.docker.internal --link hdms-postgres:postgres postgres:15-alpine \ docker: Error response from daemon: failed to set up container networking: network host.docker.internal not found Run 'docker run --help' for more information PS C:\Users\HP\Documents\INSA\hdms---help-desk-management-system (1)> psql -h hdms-postgres -U postgres -d hdms psql : The term 'psql' is not recognized as the name of a cmdlet, function, script file, or operable program. Check the spelling of the name, or if a path was included, verify that the path is correct and try again. At line:1 char:5 + psql -h hdms-postgres -U postgres -d hdms + ~~~~ + CategoryInfo : ObjectNotFound: (psql:String) [], CommandNotFound Exception + FullyQualifiedErrorId : CommandNotFoundException

ChatGPT said:

Ah! Now I see exactly what’s happening. Let me clarify:

  1. host.docker.internal is not a Docker network—it’s just a special hostname Docker provides so a container or Windows host can talk to each other. You cannot use --network host.docker.internal, that’s why Docker complained.

  2. psql is not installed on your Windows host, so running psql directly in PowerShell fails.

We need a method that actually works with your current setup.

✅ Quick way to run psql inside the container

You already have the container running (hdms-postgres). You can run the Postgres CLI inside the container itself:

PowerShell
docker exec -it hdms-postgres psql -U postgres -d hdms

  • This opens a psql prompt inside the container, no installation needed on Windows.

  • Password is already set as postgres.

Example:

SQL
hdms=# \dt         -- lists tables
hdms=# SELECT NOW(); -- checks current timestamp

Optional: Run psql from Windows without installing Postgres

If you really want to run psql from PowerShell, you have two options:

  1. Install Postgres client only:

    • Download installer: Postgres for Windows

    • During install, choose Command Line Tools only.

    • Add bin folder (where psql.exe lives) to your PATH.

  2. Use a temporary Postgres Docker container as client:

PowerShell
docker run -it --rm --link hdms-postgres:postgres postgres:15-alpine psql -h postgres -U postgres -d hdms

  • --link hdms-postgres:postgres allows this container to talk to your running Postgres.

  • Enter password: postgres

If you want, I can give you the exact PowerShell commands sequence to reset everything so your Node.js app can connect to the Postgres Docker container without editing pg_hba.conf manually, so it works immediately.

Do you want me to do that?


You said:


ChatGPT said:

Ah, I see what you’re doing in your screenshot — this is the PostgreSQL Stack Builder on Windows, and it’s prompting to download pgBouncer, which is a connection pooler. That’s fine, but for your immediate issue, you don’t need pgBouncer.

What you actually need is the PostgreSQL client (psql) installed on Windows, so you can run SQL commands directly from PowerShell instead of always going into the Docker container. That will make testing your Node.js app much easier.

Steps to Install PostgreSQL Client Only

  1. Run the installer you have (postgresql-x.x.x-windows.exe) and in the Stack Builder step:

    • Skip pgBouncer — just close that or deselect it.

    • Proceed to install Command Line Tools (psql) only.

  2. Once installed, make sure the bin folder is in your PATH. Example default path:

C:\Program Files\PostgreSQL\15\bin

  • You can add it to PATH in Windows:

    • Press Win + S, type Environment Variables, open Edit system environment variables → Environment Variables → Path → Edit → New → paste the bin path → OK.

  1. Open a new PowerShell window and test:

PowerShell
psql --version

You should see something like:

psql (PostgreSQL) 15.x

Connect to Your Docker Postgres

Once psql is available:

PowerShell
psql -h host.docker.internal -p 5432 -U postgres -d hdms

  • Password: postgres

  • Now you can run queries directly from Windows, for example:

SQL
\dt        -- list tables
SELECT NOW();  -- check connection

If you want, I can also give you a complete sequence of PowerShell commands to:

  • Reset the Docker Postgres

  • Test the connection from Node.js (ts-node test-db.ts)

  • Ensure your .env works without touching pg_hba.conf manually.

Do you want me to do that next?


You said:

our DB isn’t running; the connection is being refused on 127.0.0.1:5432 (and ::1). Fix it like this: Start a Postgres container (creates hdms DB and user): docker run --name hdms-postgres -e POSTGRES_USER=postgres -e POSTGRES_PASSWORD=postgres -e POSTGRES_DB=hdms -p 5432:5432 -d postgres:15-alpine Point the app at that container from your host by changing .env: DB_HOST=localhost DB_PORT=5432 DB_USERNAME=postgres DB_PASSWORD=postgres DB_NAME=hdms Restart the server: npm run dev. Optional quick check: npx ts-node test-db.ts (should print “DB connected!”). If a container named hdms-postgres already exists but is stopped, just docker start hdms-postgres instead of creating a new one.

ChatGPT said:

Perfect — this is the cleanest way to fix your connection issue. Let me summarize it in step-by-step actionable form so your HDMS app can connect to PostgreSQL from Windows without any extra pg_hba.conf tweaks or Docker network headaches.

1️⃣ Start a fresh Postgres container

Run this in PowerShell:

PowerShell
docker stop hdms-postgres 2>$null
docker rm hdms-postgres 2>$null
docker run --name hdms-postgres `
  -e POSTGRES_USER=postgres `
  -e POSTGRES_PASSWORD=postgres `
  -e POSTGRES_DB=hdms `
  -p 5432:5432 `
  -d postgres:15-alpine

  • -p 5432:5432 exposes Postgres to localhost on your PC.

  • The DB hdms and user postgres with password postgres are created automatically.

2️⃣ Update your .env file

Change your DB config to point at localhost instead of Docker internal IP:

env
DB_HOST=localhost
DB_PORT=5432
DB_USERNAME=postgres
DB_PASSWORD=postgres
DB_NAME=hdms
VITE_API_URL=http://localhost:3000/api
GEMINI_API_KEY=MY_GEMINI_API_KEY

This avoids no pg_hba.conf entry for host errors because Postgres now accepts connections on 127.0.0.1 by default.

3️⃣ Test the DB connection from Node

Run:

PowerShell
npx ts-node test-db.ts

  • Expected output:

✅ DB connected!

  • If it still fails, stop the container (docker stop hdms-postgres) and start it again (docker start hdms-postgres), then retry.

4️⃣ Start your app

PowerShell
npm run dev

  • Your HDMS server should now connect to Postgres correctly instead of falling back to in-memory storage.

💡 Optional quick tip: You can also test Postgres directly from PowerShell if psql is installed:

PowerShell
psql -h localhost -U postgres -d hdms

  • Password: postgres

  • Then try: \dt to list tables.

If you want, I can give you a ready-to-copy one-liner for Windows PowerShell that stops any old container, removes it, spins up a fresh Postgres, updates your .env dynamically, and tests the connection in one go. It makes this setup foolproof.

Do you want me to do that?

Messages beyond this point are only visible to you

"ServiceRequest"."description" AS "ServiceRequest_description", "ServiceRequest"."rejectionReason" AS "ServiceRequest_rejectionReason", "ServiceRequest"."createdAt" AS "ServiceRequest_createdAt", "ServiceRequest"."updatedAt" AS "ServiceRequest_updatedAt" FROM "service_requests" "ServiceRequest" WHERE (("ServiceRequest"."requesterId" = $1)) -- PARAMETERS: ["u5"] [2026-03-26T16:14:22.788Z] GET
